title: Fire Rituals for Letting Go (Without Bypassing)
dek: *Fire has been used across cultures to mark endings, but the Instagram version of letting go often skips the hardest part: actually feeling what you're releasing. Here's how to work with flame, grief, and the messy middle ground where real transformation lives, without pretending your way past the work that matters most.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Fire rituals work when they make space for genuine grief, not when they paper over unfinished emotional business with performance. Spiritual bypassing happens the moment you rush to "release" before you've fully felt what you're holding. Done well, burning transforms; done poorly, it buries what still needs attention.

What if the thing you're trying to burn away is the only part of you that's been telling the truth? We've turned "letting go" into a spiritual imperative, a pastel-filtered performance of wellness. Light a candle, write it down, watch it burn, post the ashes. But in my own practice, the ceremonies that changed me weren't the photogenic ones. They were the nights I sat with a candle and a crumpled page and sobbed for twenty minutes before I could even strike the match. The ones where I had to admit I wasn't ready. The ones where the fire revealed how tightly I was still gripping. Fire rituals can be powerful tools for closure, but only if we stop treating them like spiritual Wite-Out. Let's talk about how to work with flame in a way that honors what's real, not what looks good in a story you'll tell later. ## Why Fire Feels Like It Should Work (And When It Actually Does) Humans have always used fire to mark transitions. We've burned harvests, letters, effigies, bridges. Flame consumes and transforms in a way that feels final, irreversible, which is exactly what we crave when we're stuck in the loop of a story we can't seem to close. The symbolism is visceral: what was solid becomes smoke, ash, heat that dissipates into the night. Thomas Moore, in his work on the soul's need for ritual, reminds us that ceremony creates a container for feelings too large for ordinary conversation. Fire gives us a way to externalize an internal process, to make the invisible visible for a moment before it vanishes. That's not bypassing. That's alchemy, when it's done with full presence. But here's where it goes sideways. We perform the ritual before we've done the emotional work. We write "I release my anger" on a scrap of paper when we haven't actually let ourselves be angry yet, not really. We burn a name when we're still checking their social media every morning. We declare ourselves free when we're still carrying the weight in our shoulders, our jaw, the pit of our stomach. The fire doesn't fail us. We fail the fire by asking it to do work we haven't even started. Over the years I've seen people light a thousand candles and stay just as stuck, because the ritual became a way to avoid the feeling, not metabolize it. If you're using fire to skip ahead to the "healed" version of yourself, you're not letting go. You're just hiding. ## The Anatomy of Spiritual Bypassing in Ritual Spiritual bypassing, a term psychologist John Welwood gave us, is the use of spiritual ideas and practices to sidestep unresolved emotional pain. It's "love and light" when you need to say "I'm furious." It's premature forgiveness. It's any time you leap to transcendence before you've fully inhabited what's human and messy and unresolved. In fire rituals, bypassing sounds like this: "I'm releasing this to the universe." "I'm choosing to let go and move forward." "Everything happens for a reason." All lovely sentiments, and all potentially true, but not if you're using them as a way to shut down the part of you that's still howling. Harriet Lerner, whose work on anger and emotional honesty has shaped how we understand relational patterns, points out that real change requires us to stay in the discomfort long enough to understand what it's trying to teach us. Anger, for instance, isn't something to "release" prematurely. It's information. It tells you where a boundary was crossed, where you minimized yourself, where you need to speak up or walk away. If you burn it before you've listened to it, you'll just meet it again in a different form. Beverly Engel's research on shame and accountability adds another layer: we can't let go of what we haven't first acknowledged. If you're trying to burn away shame without naming what you're ashamed of, without tracing it back to where it started, you're just performing a ceremony over a wound that's still open. The antidote isn't to skip the ritual. It's to slow down and ask: Have I actually felt this? Have I let it move through my body? Have I said the unsayable out loud, even if only to myself? The fire can hold the ending, but you have to do the living first. ## What to Do Before You Strike the Match Here's the part no one wants to hear: the ritual is the last step, not the first. Before you burn anything, you need to sit with what you're holding. Not for an hour. For as long as it takes. Start by writing without a plan to burn. Just write. Let it be ugly, repetitive, unfair, cruel, unspiritual. Write what you actually think, not what you wish you thought. Write the things you'd never say out loud. Write until your hand aches. Write until you bore yourself. Write until you stumble onto the sentence that makes you catch your breath because it's true in a way that scares you. Then read it back. Not to judge it, but to witness it. This is the part most people skip. They write, they fold, they burn, and they never actually look at what they wrote. But the looking is the work. The looking is where you realize you've been carrying someone else's story, or a story you invented to protect yourself, or a story that was true once but isn't anymore. In our experience, the ceremonies that stick are the ones where people spend days or weeks with the material before they burn it. They carry the folded paper in a pocket. They read it out loud to a mirror. They let it sit on an altar. They make space for second thoughts, third thoughts, the return of the feeling they thought they'd already processed. That's not failure. That's depth. You might also work with your body first. Shake. Dance. Scream into a pillow. Let the emotion move as sensation before you try to translate it into words and ash. The fire will mean more if you've already started the release in your cells. And consider whether burning is even the right ritual for what you're holding. Some things need to be buried. Some need to be torn into pieces. Some need to be spoken aloud to another person before they can be symbolically released. Fire is not a one-size-fits-all solution. It's one tool among many. ## A Simple Fire Ceremony That Doesn't Skip Steps When you're ready, here's a structure that builds in the pauses that matter. Gather your materials: something to write on, something to write with, a fireproof bowl or cauldron, matches or a lighter, a cup of water nearby for safety. If you're indoors, a candle works. If you're outside, a small contained fire is better, but respect local regulations and land. Sit down and take three full breaths. Not the shallow kind. The kind that fills your belly and makes your shoulders drop. Ask yourself out loud: "Am I doing this because I'm ready, or because I think I should be ready?" If the answer is the latter, stop. Wait. There's no virtue in performing readiness. Write what you're releasing. Be specific. Not "my ex," but "the story that I wasn't enough." Not "my anger," but "the rage I've been swallowing since I was twelve because no one wanted to hear it." Specificity is kindness to your future self. Vague releases create vague results. Before you burn it, read it aloud. Let your voice crack. Let your hands shake. Let it be awkward and hard. This is the moment where the ritual becomes real, because you're not just thinking the thing, you're speaking it into the air, making it material, letting it exist outside your head for a breath before it goes. Light the edge of the paper. Watch it catch. Don't rush this part. Notice the color of the flame, the smell of the smoke, the way the paper curls and blackens. Stay present. This is not a metaphor. This is fire doing what fire does: breaking molecular bonds, releasing energy, turning matter into heat and ash. When it's done, sit with the ashes for a moment. Some traditions say to scatter them, some to bury them, some to keep them. Do what feels true. Then wash your hands, drink water, and do something ordinary. Make tea. Pet the dog. [Browse through more articles in this journal](/articles) if you need to ground. Don't immediately post about it, don't call someone to process it, don't try to assign it meaning. Let it settle. ## When the Ritual Doesn't "Work" (And What That Tells You) Sometimes you burn the thing and you still feel like shit the next morning. The person still takes up space in your head. The pattern still runs. The grief is still there, maybe even louder. This isn't failure. This is information. It might mean you burned the surface layer of something deeper. It might mean there's more to feel, more to say, more to understand. It might mean the ritual was premature, or that you need a different kind of support - therapy, bodywork, the kind of conversation that doesn't have a tidy ending. It might also mean you're in the middle of the process, not at the end. Letting go isn't a light switch. It's a spiral. You release a layer, you live for a while, you meet the next layer. The ritual marks a moment in that spiral, but it doesn't shortcut the path. One of the questions we've explored across the articles we've published on this site is the difference between ritual that transforms and ritual that performs. The difference is almost always in the willingness to stay with what's uncomfortable. If the ceremony didn't "work," maybe it worked exactly as it should: it showed you there's more to tend to. And sometimes, honestly, you just need more time. Grief has its own pace. Anger has its own arc. You can honor the timeline without judging yourself for not being "over it" yet. The fire will still be there when you're ready. It's patient that way. ## The Body Knows What You're Not Saying Your nervous system doesn't care about symbolism. It cares about safety, about whether the threat is actually gone, about whether the story you're telling matches what your body experienced. You can burn a hundred letters and still flinch when you hear their name, because the ritual happened in your mind and your body is still holding the memory. This is where the ceremony needs to become somatic, not just symbolic. Before or after the fire, you need to work with the sensations. Where do you feel this in your body? Is it a tightness in your throat, a heaviness in your chest, a knot in your gut? Can you breathe into it? Can you move with it? Try this: Stand up. Shake out your hands, your arms, your whole body like you're shaking off water. Make noise if you can - sighs, groans, whatever wants to come out. Then place one hand on your heart and one on your belly and say out loud: "I'm letting my body release what my mind has already decided to let go." Feel the warmth of your hands. Feel your breath moving under your palms. This is not woo. This is nervous system regulation, the kind of work that [the NIMH page on emotional regulation](https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/caring-for-your-mental-health) describes as essential for processing difficult emotions. Sometimes the body needs help. [Banyan Botanicals Organic Ashwagandha Tablets](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000703APK?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support your system when you're processing big feelings and your stress response is stuck on high. [MegaFood Balanced B Complex (30 tabs)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00028ONPI?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps when your nerves are frayed from emotional labor. These aren't magic bullets, but they're part of the network of support that makes deep work possible. And if you're not sleeping because the thing you're trying to release keeps you up at night, that's a signal too. [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) might help your body remember it's safe to rest while you're doing this work. You can also explore [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for other nervous system allies. The body will tell you the truth if you listen. It will tell you when the release is real and when you're just going through motions. Trust that intelligence. It's older and wiser than any ritual you can design. ## Discernment, Not Performance: Knowing When Fire Is the Right Tool Not everything needs to burn. Some things need to be spoken, witnessed, forgiven in a conversation. Some things need to be written and kept, not destroyed. Some things need to be held in therapy for months before any ritual makes sense. Some things need to be danced, or painted, or sung. Fire is for what's ready to transform. It's for what you've already metabolized enough that you can bear to watch it go. It's not for what you're still actively using to define yourself, even if that definition hurts. It's not for what you haven't yet fully felt. Ask yourself: If I burn this, am I doing it because I'm done, or because I'm trying to force myself to be done? If it's the latter, put the matches down. Go [take one of the assessments](/assessments) to get clearer on where you actually are. Go to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) and read something that reminds you slowness is sacred. Go for a walk. Come back when the answer changes. The ritual is not the goal. The ritual is a tool in service of something bigger: your wholeness, your honesty, your willingness to let life move through you instead of around you. Sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is not perform the ceremony, but admit you're not ready. That's not weakness. That's integrity. ## Three Questions People Actually Ask About Fire Rituals ### Can I do a fire ritual for someone else's behavior or for something I can't control? You can, but be clear about what you're releasing. You're not burning the other person's choices or the situation itself - you're burning your attachment to controlling it, your story about what it means, your hope that it will be different than it is. Write what you're releasing in first person: "I release my need for them to apologize," not "I release their refusal to apologize." The ritual is about your relationship to the thing, not the thing itself. ### What if I feel worse after the ritual, not better? That's often a sign the ritual cracked something open. You might be meeting a layer of grief you'd been keeping at bay, or realizing the loss is bigger than you wanted to admit. Don't pathologize the feeling. Sit with it. Journal. Move your body. Reach out to someone you trust. The "worse" might be the beginning of real processing, not a sign you did something wrong. Give it a few days before you decide what it means. ### How do I know if I'm spiritually bypassing or actually healing? Check your body and your behavior. If you feel lighter, more spacious, more able to engage with your life without reactivity, the ritual probably served you. If you feel numb, disconnected, or if you're still obsessively thinking about the thing you "released," you might have skipped steps. Real healing doesn't require you to pretend the pain wasn't real or that you're "above" your feelings. It asks you to move through them, not around them. --- Fire doesn't care about your intentions. It just burns. It takes what you give it and turns it to heat and ash, indifferent to whether you were ready, whether you meant it, whether you posted about it later. That's the gift and the terror of working with an element that can't be controlled or performed for. The question isn't whether fire rituals work. It's whether you're willing to bring the kind of honesty to the flame that makes transformation possible. Not the Instagram version of honesty, but the kind that makes your hands shake and your voice crack. The kind that admits you're not as healed as you pretend to be, not as ready as you wish you were. The kind that doesn't ask for a tidy ending, just a moment of truth. That's the offering the fire actually wants. Everything else is just smoke. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on fire rituals for letting go (without bypassing), we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
